title: Dog Nail Bed Swollen With Pus: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's nail bed swollen or leaking pus? Learn about paronychia, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
What is causing the swelling and pus?

When a dog's nail bed is swollen and discharging pus, it is typically a condition known as paronychia. This is an inflammatory infection of the soft tissue surrounding the claw. Common causes include bacterial or fungal infections, often triggered by a minor puncture, a broken nail, or chronic moisture from excessive licking due to allergies.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While a swollen nail bed is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is highly painful and indicates an active infection. You should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours. Delaying treatment can allow the infection to spread to the digital bone (osteomyelitis), which may require more aggressive treatment or even toe amputation.

Why a photo helps with triage

Providing a high-quality photo of the affected paw is incredibly helpful for remote triage. A veterinarian or technician can examine the image to check for signs of a foreign body, the angle of a potential nail fracture, or the extent of the redness. This visual information helps determine if your pet needs to be squeezed in for an immediate appointment or if the situation can wait until a scheduled slot the following day.

faqs.question: Can I drain the pus at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze or drain an infected nail bed at home. This can cause the infection to push deeper into the tissue and causes significant pain to your dog.

faqs.question: What can I do for my dog while waiting for the vet?
faqs.answer: You can perform a gentle soak using warm water and unscented Epsom salts for 5-10 minutes to help soothe the area. Ensure you dry the paw thoroughly afterward and prevent your dog from licking the site.

faqs.question: Will my dog need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: In most cases where pus is present, a veterinarian will prescribe a course of oral antibiotics or antifungal medication, and potentially anti-inflammatory drugs to manage the pain and swelling.
